---
title: "RL and Bounded Rollout Theory"
phase: thesis
audience: public
status: current
owner: jan
format: html
---

# RL and Bounded Rollout Theory {#rl-planning-theory}

ARIA-NBV should formulate its first non-myopic experiment as finite-horizon planning over discrete candidate views, not as full continuous reinforcement learning. At time $t$, the {{< gls rollout-state >}} contains the accumulated geometry-first reconstruction state, target descriptor, finite candidate table, validity masks, and any actor-visible diagnostic fields that can be obtained without privileged ground truth. Oracle labels, GT meshes, all-candidate GT renders, and GT OBB crops define supervision and evaluation; they should not become actor-visible inputs in the first rollout state.

## State and Action Space Contract {#state-and-action-space-contract}

The state contract separates what ARIA-NBV can observe from what the ASE mesh oracle may use to create labels. The notation uses explicit variants instead of a generic egocentric/counterfactual alias:

$$
s_t^{\mathrm{hist}},\quad
s_t^{\mathrm{off}},\quad
s_t^{\mathrm{cf0}},\quad
s_t^{\mathrm{cf+}},\quad
s_t^{\mathrm{oracle}}.
$$

| State variant | Role | Modalities and boundary |
|---|---|---|
| {{< gls historic-snippet-state >}} $s_t^{\mathrm{hist}}$ | Raw actor-visible state from the logged ASE/Project Aria trajectory. | Camera streams, calibration, timestamps, trajectory/gravity, semidense points with uncertainty/support, EVL/EFM evidence, and detected or predicted OBBs. No GT mesh or GT OBB is an actor input. |
| {{< gls persisted-offline-state >}} $s_t^{\mathrm{off}}$ | Compact immutable state used by the current VIN offline sample store. | `VinSnippetView`, candidate poses/cameras/counts, oracle metrics as labels, optional depths, compact OBBs, trajectory metadata, and selected EVL numeric fields. It is a training/diagnostic payload, not the full raw snippet. |
| {{< gls minimal-counterfactual-state >}} $s_t^{\mathrm{cf0}}$ | Main thesis-core actor input for target-conditioned rollouts and $Q_H$. | Frozen root EVL context, fused point proxy $P_t$, selected-view history, target descriptor, budget, candidate table, validity masks/reason codes, and current-state candidate-query features. EVL is not recomputed after synthetic observations. |
| {{< gls geometry-rich-counterfactual-state >}} $s_t^{\mathrm{cf+}}$ | Explicit ablation state. | $s_t^{\mathrm{cf0}}$ plus selected prior synthetic observations only: rendered depth, valid mask, backprojected point cloud, and derived local normals/support summaries. Unselected-candidate GT renders remain hidden. |
| {{< gls oracle-rollout-state >}} $s_t^{\mathrm{oracle}}$ | Privileged label/evaluation state. | GT mesh and target crops, all-candidate renders/points/normals/mesh-face visibility, RRI/CD metrics, oracle scores, and GT OBBs. This state supports oracle labels, upper bounds, and diagnostics only. |

The finite {{< gls finite-candidate-action-set >}} is an index set over the candidate table:

$$
Q_t=\{q_{t,i}\}_{i=1}^{N_t},\qquad
a_t\in\mathcal{A}(s_t)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,N_t\}:m_{t,i}=1\},\qquad
q_t=q_{t,a_t}.
$$

This matters because validity is a hard feasibility mask. Collision, no-depth, outside-bounds, unsupported-target, and outside-EVL-extent cases should stay in masks and reason codes, not be converted into low RRI labels.

The {{< gls counterfactual-transition >}} updates the actor-visible state only after selecting an admissible candidate index. For the ASE mesh-supervised loop, the oracle may render the selected pose to perform the transition,

$$
o_{t+1}^{\mathrm{sel}}=\mathcal{G}(M_{\mathrm{GT}},q_{t,a_t}),
\qquad
P_{t+1}=P_t\cup P(o_{t+1}^{\mathrm{sel}},q_{t,a_t}),
$$

but all-candidate GT renders and all-candidate target-RRI labels are oracle-only fields before selection. The main $Q_H$ actor input is therefore

$$
Q_H(s_t^{\mathrm{cf0}},a_t)
\approx
\mathbb{E}\!\left[
  \sum_{k=0}^{H-1-t}\gamma^k r_{t+k}^{e}
  \mid s_t=s_t^{\mathrm{cf0}},a_t
\right],
$$

with $s_t^{\mathrm{cf+}}$ reserved for a geometry-rich ablation.

The one-step greedy baseline is the depth-one selector
$$
\operatorname{ArgTop1}_1(s_t) =
\arg\max_{i \in \mathcal{A}(s_t)} r(s_t, q_{t,i}),
$$
where $r$ is the trusted oracle RRI during the first planning experiment. A bounded non-myopic rollout first restricts the search tree to the $K$ strongest valid branches,
$$
\operatorname{ArgTopK}(s_t) \subset \mathcal{A}(s_t),
$$
then computes a finite-horizon value over that pruned branch set:
$$
V_h(s_t) =
\max_{i \in \operatorname{ArgTopK}(s_t)}
\left[
r(s_t, q_{t,i}) + V_{h-1}(T(s_t, i))
\right].
$$
The selected action at horizon $h$ is therefore
$$
\operatorname{ArgTop1}_h(s_t) =
\arg\max_{i \in \operatorname{ArgTopK}(s_t)}
\left[
r(s_t, q_{t,i}) + V_{h-1}(T(s_t, i))
\right].
$$
This creates the experimental ladder $\operatorname{ArgTopK} \rightarrow \operatorname{ArgTop1}_1 \rightarrow \operatorname{ArgTop1}_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \operatorname{ArgTop1}_H$. The scientific question is whether the first action chosen by an $h$-step oracle rollout improves cumulative RRI over the depth-one greedy action under the same candidate budget, horizon, and acquisition cost.

This formulation is deliberately close to sequence decoding. Trajectory Transformer shows that offline control can be reframed as sequence modeling and decoded with beam search over predicted high-reward trajectories [@TrajectoryTransformer-janner2021]. ARIA-NBV does not need to train such a model first, because the current counterfactual evaluator already supplies typed transitions and oracle candidate rewards. The relevant import is the planning abstraction: beam width, context, and horizon are explicit computational budgets, which matches the roadmap's need for inspectable non-myopic evaluation before learned rollout claims.

Gumbel-Top-k gives the natural stochastic extension of the deterministic branch set [@GumbelTopK-kool2019]. Deterministic top-k is best for the first trust experiment because it is reproducible and easy to inspect in Rerun. Once the oracle rollout path is stable, Gumbel-Top-k can sample candidate branches without replacement from a score distribution, preserving diversity while still favoring high-utility candidates. In ARIA-NBV terms, it should be treated as a stochastic beam proposal mechanism over candidate-view sequences, not as a replacement reward.

The reward used for multi-step return should differ from the one-step training label only where the return semantics require it. The one-step VIN label remains the current RRI, because it is tied to the existing offline store and ranking diagnostics. The thesis-core return is cumulative target RRI under an equal acquisition budget,
$$
G_t^{(H)} = \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} \gamma^k r_{t+k}^e,
\qquad
r_t^e = \mathrm{RRI}_e(q_{t,a_t} \mid P_t, M_e),
$$
with $\gamma$ kept symbolic in the notation and cumulative target RRI used as the main comparison. Episode-normalized additive rewards and log-improvement rewards such as
$$
r_t^{\log,e} = \log(d(P_t^e, M_e)+\epsilon) - \log(d(P_{t+1}^e, M_e)+\epsilon)
$$
remain follow-up ablations for scale control or telescoping analysis. Scene-level RRI remains a parallel diagnostic.

Value learning should remain behind an evidence gate. If a masked discrete Q-function is trained over candidate views, Double Q-learning is the relevant safeguard against overoptimistic candidate values, because it separates action selection from action evaluation in the bootstrap target [@DoubleDQN-vanHasselt2015]. If the training data is a fixed rollout store, IQL is the stronger offline-RL reference because it avoids evaluating unseen actions while still performing multi-step dynamic programming through expectile value learning and advantage-weighted policy extraction [@IQL-kostrikov2021]. In both cases, the core ARIA-NBV constraint is support: the learned value or policy must respect sampled candidate geometry and validity masks before it is allowed to generalize beyond the offline store.

Continuous policies are a later stage, but the literature clarifies what such a stage should look like. GenNBV demonstrates end-to-end PPO over five-degree-of-freedom actions with coverage rewards and multi-source state embeddings [@GenNBV-chen2024]. Hestia improves the continuous-control formulation by factorizing action prediction into a look-at proposal and feasible camera placement, adding directional voxel-face visibility, and using a close-greedy low-discount reward to reduce spurious long-horizon credit assignment [@Hestia-lu2026]. Deep energy-based policies and soft Q-learning explain how a policy can represent multiple good actions through a maximum-entropy energy distribution [@DeepEnergyPolicies-haarnoja2017]. These ideas are useful extensions, but they become thesis-grade only after bounded oracle-RRI rollouts demonstrate real headroom over one-step greedy selection.
